summary: Solid early‑Dead set with Cream Puff War, Viola Lee Blues & tight Phil bass
blurb: The show features a forward‑moving first set highlighted by a standout Cream Puff War and a fiery Viola Lee Blues. Phil Lesh delivers a powerful intro that anchors the band, while Jerry’s vocals shine on Hey Little One. The GEMS soundboard capture is crystal‑clear and modern‑sounding.
review:
On July 30, 1966, the Grateful Dead took the stage at Vancouver's P.N.E. Garden Auditorium for a concise nine‑song set that captures the raw energy of their early period. While the official setlist runs from "Dancin' In The Street" to "New Minglewood Blues," reviewers consistently point out the real gems of the night – a blistering rendition of "Cream Puff War" and an expansive, blues‑driven "Viola Lee Blues" that pushed the band further than they had gone the previous evening. Phil Lesh’s bass intro earned the nickname “Phil bomb,” providing a solid foundation for Jerry Garcia’s soaring vocals (especially on "Hey Little One") and Bob Weir’s rhythm work. Pigpen’s vocal contribution drew mixed opinions, but his presence rounds out the early‑Dead sound.

Two soundboard sources document the performance; the GEMS recording (gd1966-07-30.sbd.GEMS.94631.flac16) stands out for its excellent, crystal‑clear mix that makes the 1966 tape feel surprisingly modern. A second SBD capture offers good clarity but suffers from minor wow & flutter and missing tracks. Together they confirm a show that, despite some recording imperfections, delivers tight playing, historic set flow, and moments that hint at the improvisational heights the band would later reach.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Both recordings describe mixed sentiment but lean positive: standout songs (Cream Puff War, Viola Lee Blues, Hey Little One) are praised, Phil’s intro is highlighted as a strong point, and the soundboard quality is noted as excellent. Only minor negatives (Pigpen vocal comment, missing tracks) appear, resulting in a modest +0.3 adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear soundboard capture with excellent mix; modern polish makes the historic performance most listenable.

key_highlights: Stellar Cream Puff War and expansive Viola Lee Blues showcase early improvisational fire.
"Phil bomb" bass intro provides a powerful anchor for the set.
Jerry’s vocal wailing on Hey Little One stands out amid the raw 1966 vibe.
GEMS soundboard recording delivers modern‑sounding clarity despite its age.

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers powerful wailing on Hey Little One and solid vocal presence throughout.
band_performance.Phil: Turns in a "Phil bomb" with an impressive intro that anchors the set.
